sections.title: Social News, Last Winter Club Night This Thursday
sections.content:
Stef Sykes, Social Secretary reports

The 6th of March sees our penultimate social and the last one in winter, which, of course, means that April will bring fine weather, warm air masses and other superb conditions.

This month Chief Coach Pete Logan will provide a cross-country oversight of three popular sites in the Dales. For those  looking to begin their journey into XC, or for those who wish to learn the tips from the Gods, do come along with your instruments and your questions.

Horse and Farrier in Otley from 8pm This Thursday 6th of March

…but get there early to make sure you have a seat.

Finally, if you have a good wheeze for our final social of the year, in April, please let me know as currently it sits unfilled.

Library on site, so bring your books and DVDs back.

sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

No committee meeting last month so you’re going to get another download of my Army Flying Career.  I’m afraid that I’ve had to give up on providing links to previous chapters - I just couldn’t get them to work reliably.  Most are available from the Skywords Archive, but I don’t suppose that any of you are really that interested.

Army Pilots’ Course – Fixed Wing

There are tales of young RAF Officers ‘holding’ for years before being loaded onto a pilots’ course.  The Army was different.  Two months after passing ‘Grading’, having handed over my job as a ‘Happy Hooker’ and moved house, I found myself reporting for Army Pilots’ Course (APC) 342 at the School of Army Aviation in Middle Wallop.  Rumour had it that, whilst it cost £2.5M to train an RAF pilot, it only cost £250K to train an Army pilot.  Draw your own conclusions!  (The system has since changed and all 3 services now train together at Shawbury.)

For those of you that favour film to my ramblings, the BBC made a documentary series (and book) covering the progress of APC 354, about 2 years after my course.  (If the link doesn’t work, search for Flying Soldiers on Youtube – this article mirrors episode 1.)

Like all Army courses the APC started off with a basic fitness test, which I’m pleased to say that I passed without too much difficulty.  Next followed a trip to the Quartermaster’s Store where we drew some unfamiliar items – a pair of, oh so soft flying boots (we’d worn trainers for grading), an aircrew watch which contained the luxury of a stopwatch, and a flight computer which was used for time/distance calculations (no batteries required – think circular slide rule).  We all donned our best bib and tucker for the course photograph.  A print out of this photo was distributed to every department and people took great delight in putting a big black cross over the faces of those departing the course prematurely.  As all good photographers know the subjects should face into the light, and it just so happened that it was sunny at the appointed time.  We all squinted into the lens, quickly cementing a course nickname: The F’uglies.

Back into the Chipmunk and I was glad that it had only been 2 months since my last flight, because we had another 5 hours until we were expected to go solo.  To clear that hurdle we had to demonstrate that we could safely take-off, complete an accurate circuit, and land; and that you had a fair chance of survival if the engine failed at any stage.  I really struggled with my landings.  I imagine that it’s much like landing a hang glider.  You fly close to the ground slowly bleeding off airspeed and then as you’re approaching the stall speed a final flare sees your wheels kiss the ground gently.  That’s the theory but, height and timing are everything.  Flare too early and you zoom back up again; flare too late and you bounce back into the sky.  I struggled to judge my height and about half of my landings tested the shock absorbers in the undercarriage.  My instructor explained that I was struggling to learn this new skill because of my advancing years – I was 29 at the time!  Perhaps thinking that it wouldn’t matter too much in a helicopter, he decided that I was worth the risk.  

Photo: Yours truly queuing up for take-off - SOLO!

Once clear of that hurdle there followed navigation exercises.  No GPS in those days and it was all done by dead reckoning: compass bearing, wind, speed and time.  If the turn point didn’t appear then you were a bit knackered so, especially for solo pilots, we could ask ATC for a STEER – a heading to bring us back to the airfield.  In order to keep a check on progress we also had to report each turn point over the radio.  One such turn point was at a place called MERE (the town near the paragliding site) which resulted in the following hilarious exchange between ATC and one of my colleagues:

AAC 123: STEER

ATC: Roger MERE

AAC 123: No, STEER

ATC: Roger, you’re at MERE

AAC 123: Negative.  SIERRA, TANGO, ECHO, ECHO, ROMEO!

We also practiced instrument flying.  To facilitate this in the Chipmunk they whitewashed the inside of the cockpit.  Only the instructor, in the back, could deconflict with other traffic by looking out of the side windows!  The smell of the whitewash only added to the feeling of motion sickness whilst focussing on the dials and being bounced around.

The culmination of the fixed wing phase was the final handling test.  Somehow, I managed to scrape through.  I was so elated that, along with a few others that had also passed, we celebrated with a night out in Andover.  Feeling very much the worse for wear the next morning, I told my instructor that I wasn’t fit to fly (something that we had been encouraged to do).  ‘Rubbish’, he said, ‘It’s character building’.  Somehow, I managed to control the contents of my stomach until just after we had landed and were taxying back to dispersal.  Lesson learned: to this day I have never gone flying with a hangover.

Our course started off with 6 Officers (including a doctor), 2 Sergeants and 5 Corporals.  This was very different from the RAF where only Officers are allowed to fly.  One of the Corporals was the first Gurkha soldier to be accepted onto the course.  His name was Pim Bahadur Gurung and he quickly acquired the nickname of Douglas Bahadur (get it?).

If you struggled to reach the minimum standard at any stage you were put onto ‘review’.  If 3 hours of remedial flying training didn’t bring you up to standard then you were removed from the course.  Individuals who suffered injuries or had compassionate problems could be ‘back coursed’.  We ‘lost’ several people from our course, but ended up with about the same number at the end because of pilots who had been back coursed to us.

Photo: The end of the Fixed Wing phase.

And so ended my fixed wing adventure.  Helicopters next…

sections.title: Winter trip to Algo, with a bonus
sections.content:
Chris Timson reports

One of the first things I learned about paragliding is how bloody frustrating it is trying to learn in the UK. In Yorkshire, we tend to have 9 months of bad weather followed by 3 months of winter. So there’s no wonder that winter trips to southern Spain are so popular.

I tried to meet up with Mark Leavesley last year on our family holiday in Annecy. He was on one of his fly guiding trips but unfortunately I missed his group by a week but I flew on my own anyway. Being able to fly almost everyday was quite new to me. Quite amazing. I need more of this!  Anyway, Mark runs trips to Algodonales twice a year and once to Annecy. Having watched his previous videos on Youtube, I quite like his informal approach so got booked on for February this year.

I met Mark and Sian, his wife - also an instructor, and the rest of the group at Malaga airport and were ushered towards two mini buses that were waiting outside. After being bounced around roads which were probably worse than ours back home, we arrived at Civa in Algodonales.

I don’t look as ill as I felt after that bus journey!

https://www.civalgodonales.com/project/alojamiento

It’s a great place to stay as they provide transport to any of the launches. You can even park your campervan there.

Anyway, an early night and the next day were greeted with brilliant sunshine and about 18 degrees. After breakfast they bused us up to the Levante launch.

Mark is very good at building confidence. Most of the group were very low hour pilots and he is very good at keeping his eye on us all. Once in the air he seems to be able to guide you on the radio to thermals as if he can see them. Obviously the vultures were quite helpful as well but he seemed to help everyone really well while Sian was helping on the ground with the setup, rigging and ground handling. At one point I was miles from launch but it was almost like he was with me showing the way.

The next day, 3 of us followed Mark on a mini XC. There was plenty of lift everywhere but great practice thermalling with the vultures. The rest of the week tended to start with a top to bottom especially at a new launch site followed by a small triangle and trying to build on this. The drivers from Civa were on hand as well and helpful sorting tangles out etc.

Got a bit excited after another day’s flying

As a bonus, I managed to sit my Pilot exam so prearranged this with Mark beforehand. I didn’t feel at my best and a bit knackered after 5 days flying but sat it in the evening while the rest of the group were noisily getting drunk outside!

I guess I was quite lucky. Only one day was too windy for flying so I did a day ground handling. At least I felt I’d done some exercise. It’s really cheap out there for food etc and quiet as well as it’s not really a holiday destination unless you have a paraglider. So ideal for low hours pilots providing us with some gentle thermals, I can see why it’s so popular. Quite different from my experience at Annecy.

Back to the UK and our weather, totally unsuitable for our hobby. Day after day of rain and wind. Is there a worse country in the world to learn paragliding?  I’ve yet to get the wing out of the bag since I got back but at least I learned that I passed my Pilot exam! Phew!

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

9 tips to get ready for the new paragliding season

Thinking about the first flights of the year, I came across this below from the team at Flyeo.  I couldn’t put it better myself and it’s an opportunity to also help promote them too - found here: https://flyeo.com

You’re thinking about your first flights of the season after the winter break. To get the year off to the best possible start, Flyeo offers ten tips to help you prepare for those first flights.

Despite the weather oscillating between summer and winter at the moment in our Northern Alps, the dates on the calendar and the first cross-country flights logged are not lying: the new paragliding season is on the horizon!

Conditions generally change fairly quickly from light to epic in May. As for pilots, they have often had little opportunity to get under their wings, having swapped them for skis or other sporting activities in winter.  So we asked our team of instructors for their advice on how best to prepare for the new season.

Check your gear

There’s a good chance your gear has spent the winter in the cupboard, more or less in the same condition as when you packed it up after your last outing. Even if that’s not the case, it’s a good idea to have a look at it before the start of the season:

Start with your wing. Check the leading edge, the upper surface, the inside of the cells, especially the seams and the internal structure. Small tears are possible, they can simply be repaired using the materials supplied when you bought the glider. Look at the risers, line by line. Are they fraying? What about the links on each line, are they still tight?

It’s recommended that you have a glider inspection every two years or 100 hours of flying. If you’re close to it, think about calling in a professional, who will also be able to readjust the lines if necessary. In Annecy, we work and recommend Certika.

While you’re at it, why not wipe off that muddy stain you’ve had since last season? It’s nice to start the new year with a clean canopy!

Then move on to your harness. Same thing, are there any tears that need repairing? What about the straps? If you haven’t had a chance to go on a gantry to readjust them, now might be the time…

Then into your flight instruments. Maybe there is a software update you’ve been ignoring for a few months? Or that new configuration your mate told you about that you want to try out.

Finally, go through all the rest of your equipment. Repair, clean, adjust or replace as necessary. It’s best to do all this at the start of the season.

Repack your reserve (or get a new one)

What about your reserve parachute? Depending on the brand, it’s recommended that you repack it every 6 or 12 months. The start of the season is ideal – you might as well start it with a freshly packed reserve!

Most paragliding schools offer a rescue repacking service. This requires a professional with the necessary skills and knowledge to take care of it. Flyeo, in particular, offers repacking services for all types of rescue, whether round, square or steerable.  Similarly, most paragliding clubs also offer a rescue packing day where all members have the opportunity to pack their rescue under the supervision of a qualified instructor. This is an opportunity to repack your rescue for free or at a lower cost.

It should be noted that reserve parachutes, even if they are not thrown, have a fixed lifespan, typically 10 years. After 5 or 6 years, you should consider selling an old model that may still have a few years of life left in it. Like all paragliding equipment, this product is of course evolving rapidly. The latest models are more compact and lighter, with faster opening times and slower descent speeds. So, if you can afford it, why not upgrade to a newer, higher-performance model?

 

If in doubt, find out how to pack your rescue parachute.

Consider a first outing dedicated to ground handling to get your sensations back

As the saying goes: ‘One hour of groundhandling equals two hours of flying’. Before taking your first flight, even if you are craving it, it’s a good idea to spend some time ground handling to get a feel for how your paraglider behaves.  On take-off, even in strong winds, you won’t have any issue. And in flight, this ground handling session will have reminded you what to expect from your glider.

Re-familiarise yourself with your local flighting site

Similarly, if you haven’t flown since last season, take a moment to re-familiarise yourself with your flying site. Remember its specific characteristics, which wind directions it’s perfect for, and which require more sustained attention. Are there any changes to the landing site, required approach, new buildings that affect the aerology?

In spring in particular, some birds may be nesting, leading to restrictions on certain flying areas. Find out before you take off!

Be ready for strong conditions in spring

Ready for your first flight of the season? Spring conditions can be very strong. The air is still fresh, so the thermal difference between the ground, which warms up quickly under the strong spring sunshine, and this fresh air can lead to costal thermals! What’s more, if the conditions are unstable and the ceiling high, it could be smoky!

If in doubt, and if it’s your first flight of the season, wait until things calm down in the middle of the afternoon… There’s no need to scare yourself on your first flight.

Don’t wait for the perfect day

If the conditions aren’t great, there’s no point in waiting for ‘the perfect day’ either. It doesn’t exist. And the best days, early in the season, will certainly be those that see the most pilots as anxious as you are to fly. You might as well avoid the crowds on the big days and all the associated risks and frustrations.

It’s better to take it easy on those first few flights and get back into the swing of things quickly. Finish adjusting your equipment so that your first big flight takes place in the best possible conditions.

Fly with other pilots

If you have a group of pilots with whom you get together to fly from time to time, it’s a good idea to suggest that the first flights of the season be done together. Why? Because it’s easier to motivate yourself as a group, discuss your doubts and review the basics together.  It’s also easier to set objectives, decide on a flight plan and, together, try to achieve them.

And it’s nice to have a debrief after the flight, over refreshments, to discuss the experience and what could be done differently on the next outing.

Choose what you are wearing wisely

The sun may be shining and it feels decidingly mild… almost summer-like. You might be tempted to pack light clothes, even swap your winter gloves for the lighter summer ones.  But be warned, it’s highly likely that once you’re up in the air, and even more so as you gain altitude, it is rather cold. If the conditions are unstable, the temperature will, by definition, fall quickly with height. It would be a shame to have to land because you’re cold!

Our advice is to plan your first flights as you would at the end of the season. It’s better to be too warm on take-off and open your jacket than the other way round. But don’t forget your sunglasses. And sun cream too!

Eat and hydrate properly before take-off and during the flight.

In the spirit of being well prepared for any eventuality, make sure you don’t take off hungry or dehydrated. You’ll appreciate not running out of energy or concentration.

If the conditions are right and the flight gets longer, make sure you have enough water to drink and even something to eat to stay on top of your game and continue your cross-country flight.

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

Congratulations to Chris Timson who got his Pilot rating last month out in Algodonales with Mark Leavsley…. And welcome back to the drizzle 🙂

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Stephen Craven, Southern Sites Officer reports

Windbank

We have changed the parking instructions for Windbank.  I have updated the site guide on our website

(https://www.dhpc.org.uk/site-guide/hawkswick-windbank-knipe-scar).

Please could everyone park at the lower end of the field from now on and keep well away from the woods at the top end as this is a sensitive area in the shooting season.  Thanks for your cooperation.

Kettlewell

I am pleased to announce that we now have permission to land at the Cricket pitch and neighbouring field to the North West at Kettlewell. I have updated the site guide.

sections.title: Sites Tour
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

My strategy of doing a sites’ tour in February, when there is unlikely to be much distraction from the prospect of actual flying, proved to be valid.  The forecast of sleet with a temperature of -2°C (-11°C with windchill) wasn’t particularly conducive to walking the ground either.  Nevertheless 5 hardy souls braved the elements.

Photo:  Martin Baxter, Rich Welbourne, Ian Denney, Peter Maxwell, John Dean.

The day dawned better than expected, roads were clear of snow and the cloud was only still hanging onto the highest peaks (Worlds End and Mallerstang).  We even had a bit of sunshine at Tailbridge, but the wind was biting and there was evidence of freezing rain.

 

Photo: Tailbridge looking South towards Worlds End and Mallerstang.  Note the car park remains closed.

Photo: Tailbridge looking North from what was the car park.

Photo: Freezing rain?

The back roads over Buttertubs were clear, and a brisk walk up and down Stags Fell got the blood flowing again.  Cricket pitch in Hawes for a sandwich and hot brew.  Despite what the Holfuy weather station said (frozen solid) the wind was spot on for Semer Water (SE) but about 20 mph too strong and far too cold.  Quick check of the NNW face and back to the cars for another warm up.

Spotted a herd of Belted Galloways in the take-off field at Wether Fell, which probably explains why the farmer is so keen to keep the gate closed.

 

Photo: Dodd Fell from Wether Fell (not blurred - that’s ice!).

The Cam High road was clear, and looks to have been resurfaced, all the way to Dodd Fell.  Took note of the new fence near the parking area and had a walk through the snow to the front.  Most of us had long journeys ahead of us so we gave the pub a miss and headed home.  The guys seemed to find it a useful exercise.  Thanks to Peter and John for driving.

sections.title: Library
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Your (stand-in) librarian will be man-handling the box of library books and DVDs up the stairs at the Horse and Farrier for the next club night on 6 Mar.  Feel free to browse the box unsupervised, but if you take (or return) anything then please record it on the sheet.  There is a comprehensive list of all the contents on the website here.  I also run a reserve list so if an item is out to someone else drop me a line and I’ll pester them to get it back.

How about this one reviewed by Pete Logan (a while ago)?

Thermal Flying : For Paraglider and Hang Glider Pilots by Burkhard Martens


Normally in flying there's plenty of videos being produced that are must see and do the rounds. Some skygods will be doling out tips and advice via harness cams etc which is all well and good. But up until now there's been very little in the way of the written word that pretty much captures all you need to know. As you start to go XC you (should at least) pretty much know most of Touching Cloudbase and the BHPA's Pilot Handbook is largely there for providing what you need to know to pass exams. This leaves a big gap for something to explain the art and science of cross country flying.

Thermal Flying attempts to cover this area and the consensus is that it's the best attempt to date. Originally written in German, it's 250 odd pages of wisdom drawn from the author's own flying experience and comp flyers he's talked to in his twenty or so years of flying. The hard back book breaks down into a few sections. It starts with four (ish) chapters on thermalling, technique, strategies, finding them etc. The rest of the book deals with the background to forming thermals and the science of getting between them efficiently; and weather systems. Bruce Goldsmith is the contributor of the last chapter and adds a lot of extra knowledge based on designing and flying wings for comps.

The actual style of the book I find to be a little bitty. This doesn't promote long sessions reading it and you'll start to find yourself darting about the page between text columns, hint and tips boxes, photos and diagrams. On the other hand the sections are short and to the point, which means the playstation generation won't lose interest in reading a section before they get to the end of it. This style is also great for times when a bloke needs to contemplate life for a while in the "small room".

Just on the photos and diagrams, You're not short changed, there's a minimum of three on each page as far as I can see and this really helps visualise what's being put across. And what's being put across will be genuinely helpful. You may not appreciate or remember all that was said about the polar curve but he's taken the trouble to simplify it to basic rules to follow e.g. if you're going cross wind to the next cloud a touch of bar will help since you'll be crabbing slightly. Hit 2 to 3 m/s sink on the way and you'll probably need about half bar for that. There are similar simplifications for thermal centering and lots of other stuff.

What you may think is not going to be useful is the way the book concentrates on Alpine flying. Well if you only spend a week in the Alps a year then you'll be a better flyer for knowing this stuff anyway. But also a lot of valley effects are felt in the Lakes and Scotland as well, just perhaps less so than the bigger stuff. Most of the thermal advice is going to translate well since the physics is the same, just the setting changes and will need adjusting for. One minor area that's missing is sea breezing and associated convergence which is certainly something we have to worry about from time to time.

The only major thing it's missing I'd say are the words "Don't Panic" written in large, friendly letters on the outside cover.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content:
Martin Baxter recounts

… a few snippets from our neighbouring clubs between 1989 - 1992.  This was around the time that the British Association of Parascending Clubs (BAPC) amalgamated with the British Hang-Gliding Association (BHGA) to form the BHPA.

sections.title: First of the Summer Wine.
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

Two Yorkshire lads trying different sports.

Me aged 19 with my windsurfing kit. Sorry no photos of me Hang gliding.

At the age of 19 I got my first car and passed my test. This opened up new opportunities.

My school friend from the age of seven Ian and I decided Hang gliding was dangerous enough

to have a go at and would give it a try. So, in 1981 Hang gliding was the thing to do.

I’m not sure how we came across Bob Bailey’s advert for Hang gliding lessons, perhaps from the paper or

the shop Tyke Outdoor Pursuits in Baildon where I got my first Windsurf board from.

Anyway the cost was £80.00 for a four day course and we were to meet Bob at the Trawl Bar Fish n Chip

Restaurant in Baildon at 12 noon.

On arrival there were probably four of us plus Bob. We had a general chit chat and got to know one another over fish n chips.

After this we went onto the moor. Not one of the spots we tend to use at the moment, but facing north and overlooking the Bingley road.

Some other lads joined us. They had had a couple of days experience previously.

We got the gliders out. They were reminiscent of the Peter Powel stunt kites we used to fly only scaled up, aluminium tubing and synthetic sailcloth materials with battens inserted.

After a couple of hours, we had our first bunny hops and we were in a seated position, but the slightly more

experienced students were lying flat.

Then Bob made a comment that turned out to be prophetic, he said “If you are at the scene of an accident and the glider is undamaged it means the pilot has taken the force of impact and is in a bad way, but if you are at the scene of an accident and the glider is badly damaged, then the glider has taken the impact and probably the pilot is ok.

It was not long after this that one of the more experienced pilots landed badly. He just hit the deck in a prone position without getting his feet down. It didn’t look too bad but he had hurt his arm so it fell on Ian and I to take him to the BRI. It turned out he had broken his arm in two places and our first day was cut short. There  was no damage done to the glider.

We remember another lad called Steve who was in the army and based at Catterick. He was quite short and complained his arms weren’t long enough he ran as fast as he could but had great difficulty getting airborne.

The next three days (not consecutive) were all at Windbank somewhere on the slopes where we now walk up. There was no fence in those days and I’m not sure about the woods.

I don’t think we ever saw Bob again. He had a young helper in his 20s called Dave, tall and thin who bombed around in a white Ford Escort Van and always turned up quite late in the day.

Any road, one day Ian took off from the 45 degree slope and he clipped one of the other gliders, went up vertically, nose pointing skywards which put him about 60ft above the ground. He twisted in the air and came down vertically nose first and impacted the ground. The glider was a wright off. Every pole was severely bent or broken but Ian just walked away completely unharmed.

Dave didn’t seem to be fazed by this but from then on we were all on tethers (that is a length of rope 20ft ish going from the nose to the A frame). Dave would hold the rope in front of us and run with it in his hand as we took off.

On the last day my best flight lasted about 1 minute with a height of about 100ft. I took off, turned left then right and landed safely near the road in the field (Car park).

Happy days Thanks to Bob and Dave.

That was it until 2016 a 35 year gap!

Kit of the early 80s. Got this from the internet and is a good representation of what we were doing.

sections.title: Solo South Africa Trip
sections.content:
Jordan Whittaker reports

Last year I found flights to Cape Town from the UK for under £250 return. I couldn’t say no! I’m a fairly new pilot with around 50 hours under my belt and I know a lot of people go away with tours in the winter and I thought it would be a good opportunity to maintain my currency over the winter, whilst not splashing out too much. I’m quite used to travelling on the cheap, my wife and I travel a lot for work. So I thought I’d give SA a go. All in all I paid just over £500 for 2 weeks, including a car the whole time. I went with some non-pilots too which kept costs down splitting things.

I was a bit concerned or worried at first because I’m not with any group. I did as much research as I could about the sites I would fly and just went for it!

First up, Lion’s Head. I had heard a lot about this site, that it can be a bit rough, so I was waiting for a really nice weather window, which came really quickly (not used to weather just being nice all the time). I hiked up and there were plenty of other pilots there. I asked for advice from both people at the landing field I visited earlier in the day, and from launch to get a good site assessment. The wind was slightly off but there’s so many people around and they were all super helpful laying my wing out nicer and getting me off!

Once I’d climbed up above the orographic clouds, the air was beautifully smooth. To see a brocken spectre so up close was amazing too. Thermals were popping off here or there but not getting particularly high, so it wasn’t possible to jump over from Lion’s Head to Table Mountain. Even still, it was great being so high, whilst my wife was chilling on the beach below me! Landing was nice and easy in a large field between 2 famous beaches: Camps Bay and the Clifton’s.

After this we drove 5 hours east to Wilderness where it was flyable almost every single day. Wilderness was so cool, it felt like I was in Canada, or Wales, definitely different to the rest of South Africa. We did plenty of hikes to waterfalls and I flew most days. Wilderness was really beginner friendly. Flying along the coast for miles was great, and meeting tonnes of pilots there. I made a few friends who I flew with on a few of the days on various sites.

Once again, everyone I met was incredibly friendly and helped me out loads. They gave great flying tips, great site assessments and fun challenges to do (similar to NCS). I definitely improved as a pilot and kept up that important currency! After Wilderness we returned to Cape Town where I flew some more sites and enjoyed the non-flying days too!

In conclusion, I almost definitely didn’t learn as much as I would do if I was on a proper tour, and I plan to go on some in the future to help refine my skills, but I’d highly recommend anyone to head to SA over the winter to get some flying in to keep up their hours. There was a wide variety of sites, the most amazing views ever, and a friendly flying community. It was really cheap once you get there, so I’ll be keeping my eye out for cheaper flights again in the future.

I’m happy to chat to anyone thinking about going to Cape Town and can give good tips for places to stay, sites to fly and people to meet.

sections.title: 3D Printing and paragliding
sections.content:
Andrew Nightingale reports

There are times when I’d rather fly without a flight deck, but there’s no instrument holders that

do the job as well, so I set about solving this with my 3D printer.

subsections.title: 3D printers are great
subsections.content:
3D printers work by drawing 2D layers of molten plastic on top of each other, to create the 3rd dimension. The layers are typically 0.3m thick but this can be varied; thinner layers giving smoother prints but at a slower speed. Something the size of your little finger would take about 30mins to print.

The plastic comes as a roll of filament and is available in a very wide range of colours, finishes and materials (PLA is the easiest to print but PETG, of plastic bottles fame, is used if more strength is required).

In the last year printers have lept forward in terms of speed and come down in price: an entry level printer is less than £200 and needs minimal setup.

The open source design software (Freecad) is not for the faint-hearted, but it’s fairly easy to use once you’ve watched a few youtube videos. I can recommend Mangojelly’s channel because he’s easy to understand and isn’t trying to sell anything. I watch them in DuckDuckgo because the inbuilt player strips all ads from youtube videos.

subsections.title: Vario clip
subsections.content:
I started with making a clip for my Fairhaven micro-alti. The first one sat at the bottom of my C riser, but the second one (pictured) clips onto the harness webbing to reduce the risk of tangling further. Both grip the webbing and vario tightly enough to survive a hypothetical dragging, and have much less risk of glare because they’re mounted vertically.

I made a similar clip for the phone I use for XCtrack but this hasn’t been tested and may prove more tricky given the size.

Model 1 vario clip on the C riser. Great viewing position when in flight

subsections.title: Brummel Hook keepers
subsections.content:
Keepers prevent accidental disconnection of brummel hooks and are very cheap to buy. But you can’t get them in gold. It was very satisfying going through rounds of prototypes to try and neaten the design and make something that is overengineered, but in gold.

Iterations of the Brummel hook keeper

subsections.title: Summary
subsections.content: 3D design and printing is a great hobby in itself. If you buy a 3D printer and don’t get on with it, it’s always good for a present for someone who you normally spend £200 on. If you just need a couple of gizmos making there’s a community of 3D printer enthusiasts on facebook. 3D printing is an open-source community and people are helpful to the point of overfriendliness. As proof, I’ve just searched on Thingiverse (online repository of free 3D designs) and found 5 different types of brummel hook guards. All available in gold…

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

It’s surprising how quickly one loses their edge after a layoff but a tip to regain their launching skills using A and C risers is to hold the wing at a low angle for a prolonged period of time (one minute maybe) then raising and lowering the wing.

It took me a while to get this right but found it to be a satisfying exercise.
